task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
Cody: (struggling in the net) Hey! Let me out!

(A mechanical winch begins to pull Cody up; he sees the blinking light is attached to a camera; the net rises higher into a tree platform where a cage awaits)

Cody: (being lowered into the cage) This isn't funny! Who's out there?

(A battered jeep emerges from the brush; Percival McLeach steps out, a gaunt man with a crocodile tooth necklace and weathered hat)

McLeach: (examining the camera) Well, well, well. Got ourselves a little trespasser, Joanna.

(Joanna, a large monitor lizard, crawls from the jeep and hisses at Cody)

McLeach: (to Joanna) Not supper, girl. This one's worth somethin'. (to Cody) You know what that net cost me, boy?

Cody: (defiant) I don't care! Let me go!

McLeach: (chuckling) Spirit. I like that. (checks his watch) But I ain't got time for spirit. I'm waitin' for something... bigger.

Cody: (realizing) You're waiting for Marahute.

McLeach: (freezing, then turning slowly) Now how would a scruffy little brat like you know about Marahute?

Cody: (catching himself) I... I just heard stories.

McLeach: (studying Cody) Stories. (paces) See, Joanna, the boy's heard stories. (suddenly grabs the cage bars) You ever seen her, boy? The great golden eagle?

Cody: (silent, eyes down)

McLeach: (smiling, releasing the bars) Course you have. I can see it in your eyes. That wonder. That... connection. (to Joanna) He rode her, didn't he? The little fool actually rode her.

(Joanna tilts her head, confused)

McLeach: (to Cody) Here's the thing, boy. I don't need you. I need her. And you're gonna help me get her.

Cody: Never.

McLeach: (sighing) They always say never. (pulls out a feather—Marahute's feather) Found this near my trap. She'll come looking for it. Or for you. Eagles are funny that way. Loyal. Stupid loyal.

Cody: (shocked) You can't—

McLeach: Oh, I can. And I will. (tosses the feather to Joanna, who catches it) But first, I need to know where the nest is.

Cody: (silent)

McLeach: (leaning close) Those eggs, boy. They're worth more than you can imagine. A golden eagle's young? Rarest creatures on earth. Zoos pay fortunes. Private collectors pay more.

Cody: (whispering) She'll kill you.

McLeach: (laughing) She'll try. That's why I need the nest location first. So I can take the eggs when she's... distracted.

(Cody looks away, tears forming)

McLeach: (softening his tone) Look. You help me, nobody gets hurt. The eggs go to good homes. The eagle? She flies free. I don't need her. Just the offspring.

Cody: (looking up) You're lying.

McLeach: (straightening) Am I? (to Joanna) Get the map, girl.

(Joanna retrieves a worn map from the jeep; McLeach spreads it, showing marked locations)

McLeach: See these X's? Nests I've already... visited. Empty now. Mothers relocated. Babies... cared for. I'm a conservationist, boy. In my way.

Cody: (seeing through it) You're a poacher.

McLeach: (grinning) I'm a businessman. (folds the map) And business is waiting. (to Joanna) Load him up.

(Joanna pushes the cage toward the jeep; Cody grabs the bars, desperate)

Cody: Wait! You don't need me! She won't come for me—I'm nobody!

McLeach: (pausing) Interesting. You'd sell yourself short to save her. (considering) But see, I think she would come. And I think you know she would. (climbs into the driver's seat) Which makes you valuable. Which makes this... (gestures to the trap) ...a very good day.

(The jeep engine roars; Cody looks back at the forest, hoping for Faloo, for any animal, for Marahute herself)

Cody: (whispering) Don't come. Please don't come.

McLeach: (hearing) What's that?

Cody: Nothing.

McLeach: (smiling) That's what I thought. (to Joanna) Let's go, girl. We've got a nest to find.

(The jeep rumbles away, cage bouncing in the back; Cody clutches the bars, watching his home disappear; above, unseen, a single golden feather drifts down from the sky, catching the sunlight)

McLeach: (spotting it in the rearview mirror) She's watching. Good. Let her watch.

(Cody catches the feather as it drifts through the cage bars; he closes his fist around it, feeling its warmth, its connection to her)

Cody: (determined) I'll get back. I promise.

(The jeep disappears into the dust; the forest falls silent; then, from high above, a shadow passes over the empty road—Marahute, searching, her cry echoing across the outback, unanswered)

Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter  . 

[Requirement] Write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
Taraji P. Henson Speaks Out After Son Allegedly Racially Profiled by USC Police

Academy Award-nominated actress Taraji P. Henson has come forward with disturbing allegations that her son was racially profiled by police officers near the University of Southern California campus, sparking renewed conversations about law enforcement treatment of young Black men and prompting promises of an internal investigation.

The "Empire" star revealed that her son, Marcel, was stopped by police while in the vicinity of USC, one of the nation's most prestigious universities. Henson, who has been open about her experiences as a Black mother in Hollywood, described the incident as a clear case of racial profiling—a practice where individuals are targeted for suspicion of crime based on their race or ethnicity rather than specific evidence of wrongdoing.

The incident has struck a chord with many who see it as emblematic of broader patterns of discrimination that persist despite increased national attention to police conduct and racial justice. For Henson, a prominent figure who has used her platform to advocate for various social causes, the personal nature of the alleged profiling has transformed abstract policy debates into a family matter.

The response from USC's Public Safety department has been notably swift and conciliatory. John Thomas, who heads the university's Public Safety division, made the unexpected disclosure that he himself has experienced racial profiling. This admission from a law enforcement administrator adds a layer of complexity to the institutional response, suggesting that even those within the system recognize its flaws from personal experience.

Thomas has committed to conducting his own investigation into the circumstances surrounding Marcel's stop. This internal review will examine whether proper protocols were followed and whether bias played a role in the officers' decision to initiate contact with Henson's son. The promise of investigation, while welcomed by some, also raises questions about the effectiveness of internal accountability mechanisms versus independent oversight.

The allegations come at a time when universities across the country are grappling with their relationships with surrounding communities and the role of campus police. USC, located in South Los Angeles, has historically had tense relations with neighborhood residents who have long complained about aggressive policing and gentrification pressures. The university has made efforts in recent years to improve community relations, but incidents like this threaten to undermine that progress.

For students and parents of color, Henson's experience resonates with familiar anxieties about sending children to predominantly white institutions where they may face hostility from both peers and authorities. The actress's visibility ensures that this particular incident will receive significant media attention, potentially forcing USC and other institutions to confront uncomfortable questions about campus safety practices.

Racial profiling remains a persistent problem in American policing despite being formally prohibited by law in many jurisdictions. Studies consistently show that Black and Latino drivers are stopped and searched at higher rates than white drivers, even though contraband is more frequently found on white drivers when searches are conducted. These disparities persist across geographic and institutional boundaries, affecting everyone from ordinary citizens to the children of celebrities.

The psychological impact of such encounters should not be underestimated. Research indicates that experiencing racial discrimination, including profiling by police, is associated with increased stress, anxiety, and other mental health challenges. For young people like Marcel, these encounters can shape their understanding of their place in society and their relationship with authority figures.

Henson's decision to speak publicly about the incident reflects a calculation that transparency serves the greater good, even at personal cost. Celebrity involvement in social justice issues often generates criticism about authenticity and attention-seeking, but it also ensures that important stories reach audiences who might otherwise remain unaware. The actress has previously discussed her fears about raising a Black son in America, making this revelation consistent with her established public persona.

The promised investigation by John Thomas will face scrutiny regarding its independence and thoroughness. Critics of internal police investigations argue that they frequently serve to protect officers and institutions rather than deliver genuine accountability. Transparency about findings and any disciplinary actions taken will be essential for maintaining credibility with the community USC serves.

As this story develops, it joins a larger narrative about the unfinished work of racial justice in American institutions. Whether the investigation yields meaningful reform or becomes another forgotten incident depends on sustained attention and pressure from both public figures like Henson and ordinary citizens who refuse to accept profiling as an inevitable feature of American life.

The allegations come at a time when universities across the country are grappling with their relationships with surrounding communities and the role of campus police. USC, located in South Los Angeles, has historically had tense relations with neighborhood residents who have long complained about aggressive policing and gentrification pressures. The university has made efforts in recent years to improve community relations, but incidents like this threaten to undermine that progress.

For students and parents of color, Henson's experience resonates with familiar anxieties about sending children to predominantly white institutions where they may face hostility from both peers and authorities. The actress's visibility ensures that this particular incident will receive significant media attention, potentially forcing USC and other institutions to confront uncomfortable questions about campus safety practices.

Racial profiling remains a persistent problem in American policing despite being formally prohibited by law in many jurisdictions. Studies consistently show that Black and Latino drivers are stopped and searched at higher rates than white drivers, even though contraband is more frequently found on white drivers when searches are conducted. These disparities persist across geographic and institutional boundaries, affecting everyone from ordinary citizens to the children of celebrities.

The psychological impact of such encounters should not be underestimated. Research indicates that experiencing racial discrimination, including profiling by police, is associated with increased stress, anxiety, and other mental health challenges. For young people like Marcel, these encounters can shape their understanding of their place in society and their relationship with authority figures.

Henson's decision to speak publicly about the incident reflects a calculation that transparency serves the greater good, even at personal cost. Celebrity involvement in social justice issues often generates criticism about authenticity and attention-seeking, but it also ensures that important stories reach audiences who might otherwise remain unaware. The actress has previously discussed her fears about raising a Black son in America, making this revelation consistent with her established public persona.

The promised investigation by John Thomas will face scrutiny regarding its independence and thoroughness. Critics of internal police investigations argue that they frequently serve to protect officers and institutions rather than deliver genuine accountability. Transparency about findings and any disciplinary actions taken will be essential for maintaining credibility with the community USC serves.

As this story develops, it joins a larger narrative about the unfinished work of racial justice in American institutions. Whether the investigation yields meaningful reform or becomes another forgotten incident depends on sustained attention and pressure from both public figures like Henson and ordinary citizens who refuse to accept profiling as an inevitable feature of American life.
